metadata.summary: A tactics catalog for improving activation, free-tier-to-paid, and expansion conversion in an existing PLG product. Core rules: pair every tactic with the metric it moves and a current baseline; show upgrade prompts at 80% of limit (not 100%); cap prompts to one per session and zero during the first session; name a comparable customer and quantify value in all upgrade copy.

content.title: PLG Optimization Tactics
content.summary: A tactics catalog for improving activation, free-tier-to-paid, and expansion conversion in an existing PLG product. Core rules: pair every tactic with the metric it moves and a current baseline; show upgrade prompts at 80% of limit (not 100%); cap prompts to one per session and zero during the first session; name a comparable customer and quantify value in all upgrade copy; reject "Upgrade Now" and "Premium Plan" as CTA text.

why.p: Post-PMF PLG products plateau because teams lack a structured tactic backlog tied to measurable funnel steps. This methodology provides an ICE-scored tactic index covering activation friction reduction, free-tier balance, self-serve checkout design, and expansion signal detection. ICE scoring ensures the backlog is ordered by impact × confidence × ease rather than team preference.

list.item: Existing PLG product where activation, free-tier-to-paid, or expansion conversion has plateaued.
Designing a free tier or self-serve checkout and wanting a curated friction checklist to instrument before launch.
Generating in-product upgrade copy, feature-gate messaging, and pricing-page variants.
Producing a quarterly A/B test backlog scored against the tactic idea bank.
Pre-PMF teams without measurable activation data — generic tactics distract from PMF discovery.
Pure sales-led products with ACV above ~$50K where self-serve patterns do not survive procurement.
Single-event transactions (one-shot ecommerce, ticketing) with no expansion or seat-growth surface.
Hard-regulated products (healthcare, banking) where "instant access, no approval" recommendations conflict with compliance.

rule.statement: Reduce signup to 3 fields maximum (name, email, password) with instant access. Move any additional qualification fields to a post-activation profile step.
rule.rationale: Each additional field reduces signup completion by 5-10%. 10-field forms fail ~70% of users before they experience any value.

rule.statement: Never show a blank canvas on first login. Pre-seed with sample data, templates, or a pre-filled demo object. The target "empty state time" before the user sees populated content is under 2 minutes.
rule.rationale: Empty states force users to think before they have context. Pre-seeded content reduces time-to-value and increases Day-0 activation rate by 20-40%.

rule.statement: Reveal features contextually as users take actions rather than teaching everything upfront. Gate each next capability on completing the previous one.
rule.rationale: Showing all features at once overwhelms users and dilutes focus on the core activation path. Progressive reveal increases feature adoption depth without increasing early drop-off.

example.title: Project management tool: 4-step progressive reveal tied to user actions
example.code: 1. Login → Create first task (core value demonstrated) 2. Add 3 tasks → Show task organization options 3. Organize tasks → Show collaboration features 4. Invite teammate → Show advanced reporting/analytics

rule.statement: Design the free tier to give enough value to reach the Aha moment with clear natural upgrade triggers (usage limits, feature gates, user limits) — not so generous that there is no incentive to upgrade, not so restrictive that the user cannot experience value.
rule.rationale: Too-generous free tiers produce low conversion (users never hit limits). Too-restrictive tiers produce high abandonment (users cannot see value before hitting a wall).

list.item: Feature gates: basic vs premium features — upgrade trigger is needing advanced functionality.
Usage limits: 5 projects, 100MB storage — upgrade trigger is hitting capacity at meaningful usage.
User limits: 1-2 users free vs unlimited team — upgrade trigger is team growth.
Time limits: 14-day trial of premium features — upgrade trigger is trial expiration with demonstrated value.

rule.statement: Show upgrade prompts at 80% of limit consumption, never at 100%. Cap upgrade prompts to one per session and zero during the first session. Never show during a critical workflow or immediately after a recent dismissal.
rule.rationale: Prompts at 100% arrive too late — the user has already hit a wall and is frustrated. First-session prompts break trust before value is demonstrated. Over-prompting trains users to dismiss all prompts.

rule.statement: All upgrade copy must: name a comparable customer, quantify the value (hours saved, projects unlocked), name the specific plan, and include two CTAs (primary: upgrade, secondary: see example or learn more). Reject "Get More Features", "Upgrade Now", and "Premium Plan" as CTA text.
rule.rationale: Generic upgrade copy does not connect the user's current need to the plan's value. Named customer + quantified benefit copies convert at 2-3x generic CTA text in A/B tests.

example.title: Feature gate message: team exceeds 3-member free limit
example.code: "Teams like Acme Corp save 10 hours/week with unlimited member access. Your team is ready for [Product] Team plan." [Upgrade to Team] [See what's included]

example.title: Generic upgrade prompt — no customer, no value quantification
example.code: "This feature is for Pro users only." [Upgrade]

rule.statement: Pricing pages must have: 2-4 tiers maximum, a feature comparison table, an annual discount (15-20%), and no "Contact Sales" button for basic or pro tiers. Instant access on upgrade — no approval wait.
rule.rationale: More than 4 tiers introduces choice paralysis. Hiding annual discount reduces LTV. "Contact Sales" on standard tiers is a conversion cliff that routes self-serve users into a sales process they did not want.

rule.statement: Monitor team-growth signals (multiple users from same domain, @mentions, external sharing), power-user signals (daily active usage, API usage, integration connections), and upsell-readiness signals (feature gate hits 3+ times, pricing page visits). Route PQL-qualified users to targeted upgrade prompts; do not show expansion prompts to users who have not hit these signals.
rule.rationale: PQL-based prompts sent at behavioral readiness convert at 3-5x prompts sent on time-based schedules alone.

section.name: ab-test-idea-bank
section.p: Onboarding tests: number of signup fields (2 vs 3 vs 5); social login vs email-only; interactive walkthrough vs tutorial video; empty state vs pre-filled templates; progress checklist vs progress bar.

Upgrade prompt tests: prompt timing at 70% vs 80% vs 90% of limit; prompt format (banner vs modal vs sidebar); CTA copy ("Upgrade" vs "Unlock" vs "Get unlimited"); price anchoring (monthly vs annual shown first).
Pricing page tests: 2 vs 3 vs 4 tiers; annual discount amount (15% vs 20% vs 25%); feature comparison layout (table vs cards); CTA position (top vs bottom vs both); FAQ placement.

list.item: Always pair a tactic with the activation/expansion metric it moves and a current baseline; tactics without baselines are theatre.
Run the "TOO GENEROUS / TOO RESTRICTIVE / BALANCED" free-tier sketch as an explicit subagent step before recommending any limit change.
For upgrade prompts, enforce the rules: name a comparable customer, quantify saved time/value, name the plan; reject "Upgrade Now" and "Premium Plan" copy.
Trigger PQL-based prompts at 80% of limit (per methodology), never 100% — bake this into any auto-generated flow config.
Cap upgrade prompts to one per session and zero during the first session; bake this into any auto-generated Appcues/Pendo flow.
Keep ≤4 pricing tiers and require an annual-discount field in any pricing-page draft; reject layouts that hide it.

template.purpose: Dataclass for ICE-scoring tactic backlog; bucket() returns test_now / this_quarter / if_capacity / backlog
